title: Character estimates for classical finite simple groups
speaker: Michael Larsen
abstract: This is intended to complement the recent talk of Pham Huu Tiep in this seminar but will not assume familiarity with that talk. The estimates in the title are upper bounds of the form |χ(g)|≤χ(1)α, where χ is irreducible and α depends on the size of the centralizer of g. I will briefly discuss geometric applications of such bounds, explain how probability theory can be used to reduce to the case of elements g of small centralizer, discuss the level theory of characters, and conclude with the reduction to the case of characters χ of large degree.

proof_stages.label: Geometric product problem and property P
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins with a product morphism from conjugacy classes in a simple algebraic group and formulates a field property P_k saying that sufficiently large total conjugacy-class dimension forces the identity to lie in the product.

proof_stages.label: Frobenius character-sum reduction
proof_stages.summary: For finite groups, Frobenius's formula expresses the number of identity products in given conjugacy classes as a main trivial-character term plus a sum over nontrivial irreducible characters, so proving product results is reduced to making all nontrivial character terms negligible.

proof_stages.label: Uniform exponential character estimate
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem is stated: for every finite simple group of Lie type, irreducible character chi, and nonidentity element x, |chi(x)| is bounded by chi(1) to the power 1 minus a positive constant times log|x^G|/log|G|.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to unbounded classical groups
proof_stages.summary: Exceptional and bounded-rank groups are handled by existing Gluck-type estimates, leaving the substantive work to classical groups of growing rank and closely related linear, unitary, symplectic, and orthogonal groups.

proof_stages.label: Level theory and degree comparison
proof_stages.summary: A notion of level is introduced via powers of a natural permutation character, and Lusztig/Green parametrization plus branching information give estimates relating level to log chi(1)/(n log q) for classical groups.

proof_stages.label: Regular-element large-character base case
proof_stages.summary: Using the identity sum_chi |chi(x)|^2=|C_G(x)|, the desired estimate is immediate in the quadrant where x is sufficiently regular, so its centralizer is small, and chi has large degree.

proof_stages.label: Tensor-square descent to small characters
proof_stages.summary: For low-degree characters, level theory implies that chi squared has few irreducible constituents; a descending induction on character degree then transfers the estimate from larger constituents while smaller constituents cannot contribute enough to violate the bound.

proof_stages.label: Random-product reduction from singular to regular elements
proof_stages.summary: Frobenius's formula is recast as an expectation for products of independent random conjugates, and support-growth/equidistribution estimates show that products from singular classes are usually regular enough to invoke the already established bounds.

examples.name: Small conjugacy classes in SU(2) over R
examples.note: A cluster of very small conjugacy classes together with one large class in the compact group SU(2) shows that the proposed field property cannot hold over R, forcing a more restricted finite-field/algebraically-closed formulation.
examples.engagement_reason: he uses SU(2) over R as a counterexample, drawing/arguing with small conjugacy classes plus one large class to see the identity is not hit

passes.reason: Used to show that property P fails over R, while the chosen small noncentral SU(2) conjugacy classes are ordinary rather than classification-exceptional.

examples.name: Norm-one group of a Q_p division algebra
examples.note: The same small-conjugacy-class obstruction is cited for norm-one elements in a division algebra over Q_p, illustrating that the property also fails over local fields.

examples.name: Symmetric-group levels via Young diagrams
examples.note: The familiar S_n picture—permutation character powers, sign twists, and Young diagrams with long rows or columns—is used to generate the analogous level theory for finite classical groups.
examples.engagement_reason: he develops the symmetric-group level example via permutation characters, Young diagrams, degree bounds, hook lengths, and branching by removing boxes

passes.reason: The displayed low-level Young-diagram characters include the level-zero trivial/sign base cases and lie at the low-degree edge of S_n representation theory.

examples.name: Matrix with a codimension-one non-fixed part
examples.note: A linear transformation with a very large eigenspace illustrates why one must multiply roughly n/support(x) conjugates before expecting a regular element.
examples.engagement_reason: he applies the support definition to a codimension-one non-fixed example, computing that support one forces about n copies in the random-product reduction

passes.reason: A support-one matrix is a minimal nontrivial perturbation with a hyperplane fixed, used as the enemy case showing many products may be needed for regularization.
A matrix with only a codimension-one non-fixed part has minimal nonzero support and serves as the enemy case showing many products may be needed for regularity.
